title: Towards Creating a Body of Evidence-based Interactive Digital Narrative Design Knowledge: Approaches and Challenges
authors: Roth, Christian and Koenitz, Hartmut
abstract: In the context of this paper we take design knowledge as the methods applied by the creators (for example game designers in the case of games) of interactive experiences in contrast to for example the design of authoring tools or the design of game engines and similar computational systems. Such knowledge about rule-based game design is widespread as evidenced by numerous book publications and many university-level programs of study. In contrast, there is considerable less academic or professional knowledge on the design of interactive narratives -- for example, book publications focused on this topic number in the single digits and in academic programs the topic only exists on the margins. This paper proposes the use of empirical research methods as means to address this situation and examines specific challenges. On this foundation, we introduce a research effort to gather transferable interactive narrative design knowledge and report on a first user study. Finally, we provide guidelines and discuss future research.
venue: Proceedings of the 2nd International Workshop on Multimedia Alternate Realities
primary_reason: Provides its own definition of design knowledge (methods applied by creators) and works toward synthesizing a body of interactive digital narrative design knowledge.
val_reason: The truncated abstract centers on teaching object-oriented design knowledge and related constructs like patterns, but does not clearly show whether it synthesizes or defines them, so full text is needed.

title: The Dark Patterns Knowledge Stack: Exploring New Ways to Negotiate Context, Law, and Design
authors: Gray, Colin M.
abstract: Research on dark patterns has grown rapidly, but challenges remain in situating these practices within broader socio-technical, legal, and design contexts. In this essay, I introduce the concept of the ‚Äúdark patterns knowledge stack‚Äù as a new way of synthesizing evidence about manipulative, coercive, and deceptive design practices. Inspired by Alexander‚Äôs notion of pattern language, I demonstrate how the knowledge stack aligns multiple layers of analysis and evidence‚Äîfrom interfaces and user characteristics to the socio-technical landscape and user intentions‚Äîrevealing how manipulative practices interrelate across scales, are perpetuated through key business metrics, and evolve over time. Use of the knowledge stack is demonstrated through two case studies, followed by provocations for scholars, regulators, and practitioners to work together to more effectively identify harms, negotiate accountability, and chart pathways for more just and transparent digital systems.
venue: Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Essay synthesizing dark patterns evidence into a 'knowledge stack' concept inspired by pattern language, defining its own framework, plausibly satisfying C2/C3 but warrants full-text confirmation.
val_reason: Studies tacit knowledge transmission through game design practice in a small team, but the abstract does not clearly indicate a synthesis from prior literature or an explicit definition of the construct.

title: Toward an Inventory of Plans and Patterns, and Their Use to Foster Strategic Knowledge in Programming Novices
authors: Costantini, Umberto and Lonati, Violetta and Morpurgo, Anna
abstract: When learning to program, different types of knowledge and skills have to be built: syntactic knowledge, i.e., knowledge of the language features; conceptual knowledge, i.e., knowledge of the semantics of the various constructs; strategic knowledge, i.e. the ability to use syntactic and conceptual knowledge in the most appropriate and effective way to solve programming problems. Strategic knowledge also includes the ability to exploit and combine solutions to known problems to solve new ones and is one of the hardest skills to acquire when learning to programm [5, 6, 11]. Terms such as goals[10] patterns [5, 6, 11]. schema,[9] strategies[4], and variables' roles[3] have been used to refer to this kind of knowledge. Several authors suggest that strategic knowledge should be taught explicitly, however there are three main issues to address: plans/patterns/schema are used sometimes interchangeably and sometimes similarly but not correspondingly; it is claimed that a small number of plans/patterns is needed in an introductory programming course, however there is no agreed upon inventory of plans/patterns among the CS community;the notion of variables' roles isn't always explicitly linked to the notion of plans/patterns;little instructional material is available that explicitly teaches strategies, and it is neither easy nor obvious how to design such material. The aim of this study is to investigate whether and how such notions of plans/patterns/schema and variables' roles can be integrated into introductory programming courses.The idea is to establish a framework that includes and arranges the different definitions presented in literature. The framework developed will be used for building a catalogue of plans/pattern and variables' roles encountered in CS1 practice, in CS1 assessment, and in the literature on introductory programming; this catalogue will be the basis for designing new instructional/learning material for fostering strategic knowledge. The developed material will be used in one of our introductory programming courses and in peer tutoring activities for the students. The effectiveness will be tested evaluating students programming skills with a pre-test and a post-test, using a group as a treatment group and another one as control group.
venue: Proceedings of the 2020 ACM Conference on International Computing Education Research

title: Designerly Ways of Knowing in HRI: Broadening the Scope of Design-oriented HRI Through the Concept of Intermediate-level Knowledge
authors: Lupetti, Maria Luce and Zaga, Cristina and Cila, Nazli
abstract: Interest in design methods and tools has been steadily growing in HRI. Yet, design is not acknowledged as a discipline with specific epistemology and methodology. Designerly HRI work is validated through user studies which, we argue, provide a limited account of the knowledge design produces. This paper aims to broaden current understanding of designerly HRI work and its contributions by unpacking what designerly knowledge is and how to produce it. Through a critical analysis of current HRI design literature, we identify a lack of work dedicated to understanding the conceptual implications of robotic artifacts. These, in fact, are implicit carriers of crucial HRI knowledge that can challenge established assumptions about how a robot should look, act, and be like. We conclude by discussing a set of practices desirable to legitimize designerly HRI work, and calling for further research addressing the conceptual implications designerly HRI work.

title: Let's Get Divorced: Constructing Knowledge Outcomes for Critical Design and Constructive Design Research
authors: Forlizzi, Jodi and Zimmerman, John and Hekkert, Paul and Koskinen, Ilpo
abstract: Over the last two decades, constructive design research (CDR) - more commonly called Research through Design within HCI - has become an accepted mode of scholarly inquiry within the design research community. It has been described as having three distinct genres: lab, field, and showroom. The lab and field genres typically take a pragmatic stance and typically propose a preferred future. Research done following the showroom approach - more commonly known as critical design (CD), speculative design, or design fictions - typically offers a polemic and a critique of the current state embodied in an artifact. Recently, we have observed a growing conflict within the design research community between pragmatic and critical design researchers [4]. To help reduce this conflict, we called for a divorce between CD and pragmatic CDR, advocating that each approach has its own merits and should be evaluated on its own account. Other design researchers have pushed back on this stance, seeking to create some middle ground to connect these two types of research. In this day-long workshop, we seek to look at exemplar CDR and CD case studies, to develop methods for describing, evaluating, replicating, and making use of knowledge outcomes from these two forms of design research.

title: Immodest Proposals: Research Through Design and Knowledge
authors: Bardzell, Jeffrey and Bardzell, Shaowen and Koefoed Hansen, Lone
abstract: The paper offers theoretical support for research through design (RtD) by arguing that in order to legitimize and make use of research through design as research, HCI researchers need to explore and clarify how RtD objects might contribute to knowledge. Leveraging the tradition of aesthetics in the arts and humanities, we argue that while the intentions of the object's designer are important and while annotations are a good mechanism to articulate them, the critical reception of objects is equally foundational to RtD's broader knowledge impacts within HCI. Such a scholarly critical reception is needed precisely because of the potential inexhaustibility of design objects' meanings; their inability to be paraphrased simply and adequately. Offering a multilevel analysis of the (critical) design fiction Menstruation Machine by Sputniko!, the paper explores how design objects co-produce knowledge, by working through complex design problem spaces in non-reductive ways, proposing new connections and distinctions, and embodying design ideas and processes across time and minds.
venue: Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
primary_reason: Offers theoretical support for research through design and clarifies how RtD objects contribute to knowledge, engaging with design knowledge as topic and likely defining constructs.
val_reason: The paper centrally concerns knowledge contributions of research through design and appears to theorize how RtD objects contribute to knowledge, but the truncated abstract does not clearly show a synthesis from prior work or an explicit definition, so full-text review is needed.

title: Towards scaling expertise with generative AI: design principles for governing AI-Mediated knowledge capture and transfer
authors: O‚ÄôSullivan, Philip (1); Sepp√§l√§, Selja (1); Feller, Joseph (1)
abstract: Tacit expertise (e.g. decision heuristics, risk judgement) makes organisational decision-making distinctively human, yet resides in individuals, is hard to scale, and is lost on departure. Generative AI (GenAI) offers conversational elicitation, synthesis, and adaptive scaffolding, but risks hallucination, goal drift, and helpfulness-driven agreement bias, undermining independent judgement. Building on validated problem theory, we derive four design principles grounded in socio-cognitive knowledge-work theory. They specify governance mechanisms addressing persistent transfer challenges (knowledge hiding, articulation barriers, internalisation failure) and GenAI-specific risks (knowledge distortion, tool-dependent learning, disrupted social exchange). Logical reasoning validates for alignment, applicability, coherence, completeness, and operationality. A feasibility assessment across three GenAI configurations shows most mechanisms are implementable through prompting; those requiring persistent state or platform integration are not, with configurations approximating rather than enforcing governance. We contribute prescriptive design knowledge for preserving human-centric decision-making by motivating expert sharing, enabling articulation, establishing fidelity, and ensuring applicability to successors. ¬© 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

Using the roles defined in this anatomy, the Organisation is the Implementer (I) that establishes the governance architecture and defines mechanism parameters; the Enactor is the GenAI system carrying out Mechanisms; Users (U) are Experts and Novices for whom Aims are achieved. Validation approach Tuunanen et al. (2024) recommend logical reasoning, benchmarking, expert interviews, and focus groups as validation techniques for the Objectives and Requirements Definition echelon, noting that researchers should select suitable subsets that fit their research context. Because the design principles are theoretically derived specifications, logical reasoning validates the soundness of their derivation from the Echelon 1 problem theory. Because the Mechanisms target probabilistic systems whose capabilities vary across architectures, benchmarking establishes whether they are technically feasible across current GenAI configurations. Together, these two techniques address all six validation criteria specified for this echelon. We defer expert interviews and focus groups to a consolidated practitioner engagement spanning Echelons 1 through 3, enabling practitioners to evaluate the design principles alongside both the problem theory they address and the projectable design that elaborates them. The combined session tested whether Mechanisms held when all four DPs’ Mechanism specifications were active. This design tested the ceiling of what prompting could achieve. If a mechanism could not hold when a knowledgeable researcher used clear instructions and actively tried to sustain it, it is unlikely to be feasible through prompting alone. Mechanisms were coded at three levels. Mostly implementable through careful interaction (●): the Mechanism could be carried out through prompting and maintained under bypass attempts. Partially implementable through careful interaction (◑): the Mechanism could be produced through prompting but was not sustained under bypass attempts or across the full session. Not implementable through careful interaction (○): the Mechanism could not be supported even when explicitly requested; architectural implementation may be required. Coding was conducted by the first author. A second coder independently coded a stratified random subset of four sessions (27% of the 15 coded sessions), selected to cover all three configurations and three of the four DPs plus one combined session. 2 P. O’SULLIVAN ET AL. governance that specifies the conditions under which it augments professional expertise (Chowdhury et al., 2024; Saup et al., 2026). Yet existing governance discourse centres on compliance and risk controls for AI outputs (e.g. Benbya et al., 2024), and recent decision support work defines admissibility conditions that outputs must satisfy before entering decision work (Saup et al., 2026). Both presuppose that trustworthy knowledge inputs exist. When tacit expertise is lost through turnover, no admissibility framework can govern what was never captured. Addressing this foundational gap requires governing the upstream processes through which expert knowledge is captured and preserved as an organisational resource. Accordingly, extending our prior problem theory (O’Sullivan et al., 2025) through Echeloned Design Science Research (eDSR) (Tuunanen et al., 2024), this paper derives and validates four design principles (DPs) for governing GenAI-mediated tacit knowledge transfer. Prior research Sustaining knowledge-driven decision support requires transferring expert judgement so it remains retrievable and reusable (Levallet & Chan, 2019). The SECI model (Nonaka et al., 2000) frames this as a conversion challenge across four modes: Socialisation (tacit-to-tacit), Externalisation (tacit-to-explicit), Combination (explicit-to-explicit), and Internalisation (explicit-to-tacit), each enabled by what Nonaka et al. (2000) term Ba, the shared physical, virtual, or mental space that provides context for mutual understanding, feedback, validation, and practice.

JOURNAL OF DECISION SYSTEMS 7 Design principle 3: provenance-anchored verification Context. GenAI outputs may contain hallucinations or distortions (P5), yet their fluency and speed of generation make rigorous verification difficult, while captured artefacts may contain gaps requiring refinement (P3) and expert review time is limited (P2). Aim. For the Organisation (I) to enable Experts (U) to verify that captured knowledge accurately represents their expertise, without excessive time burden. Mechanisms. Transparent source-claim traceability enables detection and correction through: (i) source-claim linking, where each assertion is hyperlinked to the dialogue turn producing it; (ii) low-friction correction, enabling reject, refine, or flag actions with minimal effort; (iii) verification audit trails, recording approvals and edits before organisational release. When the expert reviews the synthesised output, each insight appears alongside the original transcript passage it was drawn from. The synthesis captures the expert’s relationship mental model but presents it as a general comparison between customer behaviour and interpersonal dynamics. The expert recognises this preserves the analogy but omits the element that makes it useful for decisions: that different stages of the pattern map to different recommended responses. They flag this, the system revises it based on that feedback without altering content that remains accurate, and the correction is recorded before the artefact is released. Rationale. Source-claim linking operationalises Epistemic Vigilance Theory (Sperber et al., 2010): by making provenance (the origin of each claim) visible, it transforms verification from memory reconstruction to efficient, evidence-based review.

The system then organises these insights into a structured decision record capturing the context, cues, reasoning, and outcome. Rationale. Drawing on Naturalistic Decision Making, protocol gating operationalises structured probing methods that surface tacit knowledge (Hoffman et al., 1998) by requiring completion of phase-specific steps before advancing, thereby constraining goal drift and sycophancy documented in LLMs (Arike et al., 2025; Lindström et al., 2025). Deviation detection complements this by identifying shallow agreement or topic drift and triggering re-probing. Structured synthesis codifies expertise into retrievable artefacts (Hansen et al., 1999), and interaction logging preserves the dialogue context needed for subsequent verification (DP3). Feasible through agentic architectures, systems in which an LLM is programmed to execute multi-step workflows with required checkpoints (Yao et al., 2023). Design principle 2: attribution-linked reciprocity Context. Expert knowledge sharing is driven by perceived value, yet GenAI intermediation removes the interpersonal rewards that sustain it (P7), increasing the risk of knowledge hiding (P1). Aim. For the Organisation (I) to enable Experts (U) to sustain willingness to contribute knowledge in non-human interaction. Mechanisms. Making contributions visible, attributable, and practically useful through: (i) persistent authorship tagging, linking expert identity to resulting artefacts; (ii) impact feedback, providing experts with visibility into how their contributions are used; (iii) expert-facing utility artefacts, generating outputs of immediate practical value to the expert (e.g. decision aids); (iv) pattern surfacing, revealing tacit heuristics experts were unaware of.

discussion_full:
[Discussion paragraph 1] The Echelon 1 problem theory described in O’Sullivan et al. (2025), reframed GenAI-mediated transfer as a governance design challenge for a probabilistic, non-human intermediary. This paper extends that theory by deriving four DPs that operationalise its requirements as governance Mechanisms. The DPs contribute to work on GenAI’s potential for knowledge work (Alavi et al., 2024) by specifying testable Mechanisms through which conversational interaction, synthesis, and scaffolding can be governed to address documented risks (Lee et al., 2025; Lindström et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025). In doing so, they address a gap in decision support research. Recent work on embedding GenAI into strategic decision-making identifies admissibility conditions (quality and trustworthiness requirements) that outputs must satisfy before entering decision work (Saup et al., 2026). But admissibility presupposes that trustworthy knowledge inputs exist. When tacit expertise is

[Discussion paragraph 2] 10 P. O’SULLIVAN ET AL. lost through turnover (Daghfous et al., 2023; Levallet & Chan, 2019), human judgement is lost as an input, and no admissibility framework can govern what was never captured. Elicitation (DP1) and verification (DP3) address admissibility directly by governing the quality and provenance of captured knowledge. Reciprocity (DP2) and scaffolding (DP4) extend beyond it, sustaining expert contribution and capability development that such frameworks take for granted. Together, the principles position governance as an enabling condition for human-AI symbiosis (Chowdhury et al., 2024), complementing the compliance and risk concerns that current GenAI research identifies (e.g. Benbya et al., 2024). While the theoretical foundations and mechanism types are individually established, this paper proposes a connected governance set specifying how they function together as requirements for GenAI-mediated knowledge transfer. The feasibility assessment clarifies a distinction the derivation anticipated but did not resolve, showing which Mechanisms are implementable through interaction and which require architectural support. Protocol gating, competency gating, source-claim linking, and authorship tagging held consistently across all three configurations under explicit instruction. Interaction logging, impact feedback, verification audit trails, and fading scaffolds did not, each requiring persistent state or platform integration that interaction alone cannot reliably provide. The RAG configuration illustrates both what interaction can achieve and where it falls short. It was the only configuration to produce a full verification audit trail with timestamps and structured records.

[Discussion paragraph 3] However, because these records are generated text rather than system-level logs, the audit trail is itself subject to the same generation risks it is meant to detect. Conversational instruction can simulate governance outputs, but simulation is not enforcement. Deployment therefore requires system-generated audit trails, not LLM-generated approximations. Beyond this architectural distinction, GenAI is optimised for answer-provision through preference-based training (Ouyang et al., 2022). For elicitation, reciprocity, and verification (DP1–DP3), this is productive. Generating, synthesising, and structuring are consistent with how these systems are trained. But scaffolding (DP4) addresses internalisation, the process through which novices develop their own capacity for judgement. This is not a lesser form of support but a different one, oriented toward building the capacity to judge rather than providing the judgement itself. No tested configuration exhibited competency gating, effortful hooks, or progressive scaffolding without governance instruction, and in integrated sessions scaffolding (DP4) Mechanisms proved most unstable. One configuration requested a self-reported comfort level rather than requiring a problem-solving attempt, producing the appearance of compliance without satisfying the Mechanism’s intent. Governed systems will therefore require both system-level infrastructure for state management, audit, and analytics, and architectural constraints that enforce the deliberate withholding of answers where capability development requires it. This makes DP4 the principle most dependent on architectural implementation rather than interaction-level governance. Beyond mechanism feasibility, the results prompt refinements to each principle’s Context, Aim, or Rationale.

[Discussion paragraph 4] For elicitation (DP1), the Mechanisms governing the conversational elicitation process held under instruction, but interaction logging proved unreliable across configurations. The current Aim targets structured reusable artefact, but does not state that the interaction record must also be preserved for downstream verification (DP3). The benchmarking shows it is not: without a reliable interaction record, the artefact DP1 produces is incomplete as an input to DP3. Therefore, we propose refining the Aim to explicitly acknowledge this downstream dependency rather than treating artefact production as its terminal point. For reciprocity (DP2), both the Context and Rationale frame disrupted social exchange as a motivation problem, but impact feedback (providing experts with visibility into how their contributions are used) was not fully implementable across configurations. Full achievement of the Aim depends not only on motivating experts but also on infrastructure that can track and report how contributions are used. To address this, the Context should be revised to identify this infrastructure dependency, and the Rationale must be extended to account for it. For verification (DP3), the Aim of verified artefacts depends on two distinct capabilities the Context treats as one. Detecting errors can be achieved within a conversation, but recording approvals requires system-level logging that persists beyond it. We recommend refining the Context to distinguish these two capabilities, and updating the Aim to reflect that verified artefacts depend on both. For scaffolding (DP4), the results confirm the Context’s premise that GenAI defaults to providing answers rather than building capability.

[Discussion paragraph 5] However, the Aim of independent capability implicitly assumes continuity of learner state across interactions, a precondition the benchmarking shows cannot be met through conversation alone. Accordingly, the Aim should be revised to make this structural precondition explicit, rather than leaving it as an unstated assumption.

[Discussion paragraph 6] JOURNAL OF DECISION SYSTEMS 11 Several refinements to the framework’s CAMR specifications emerge when the four principles are considered together. While the derivation anticipated that the principles depend on one another, the benchmarking demonstrated that conversational interaction alone cannot sustain the persistent state required to bridge them. Because the initial Context definitions focus primarily on the conversational interface, they do not explicitly account for this structural dependency. To fully achieve the interconnected Aims, the framework requires overarching specifications detailing how state is maintained and coordinated across principles. Furthermore, because feasibility testing evaluated the principles in separate sessions, the handover between the capture-side principles (DP1 to DP3) and the learning-side principle (DP4) remained unexamined. Since this handover represents a fundamental shift in both Context (from expert to novice interaction) and Aim (from knowledge capture to capability building), subsequent design echelons must define specifications for transitioning between operating modes and routing artefacts between them. Finally, integrated testing revealed that enforcing multiple principles simultaneously can cause the system to prioritize one mechanism at the expense of another. Given that the individual Rationales justify their mechanisms in isolation, the framework requires precedence rules establishing which Aim governs when the system must choose between them.

[Discussion paragraph 7] Together, these empirically derived revisions demonstrate that governing GenAI-mediated transfer requires an integrated specification layer above the individual principles, which is particularly critical because the generative risks these principles address compound over prolonged interactions (Lindström et al., 2025). These gaps matter because of what the framework is meant to protect. Expert decision-making is patternbased and situated (Klein, 1993), and structured probing methods exist because this knowledge resists direct articulation (Hoffman et al., 1998). Without DP1’s protocol gating and deviation detection, captured expertise risks retaining stated facts but losing the contextual pattern recognition that makes it valuable for decisions. GenAI’s tendency toward fluent, agreeable responses can bypass the structured elicitation needed to surface such knowledge (Lindström et al., 2025). Capture itself depends on expert willingness to participate, which GenAI intermediation can undermine by removing the interpersonal rewards that motivate sharing. But capturing current expertise is not sufficient if its recipients cannot develop independent judgement. Humancentric AI research treats human oversight of AI outputs as a central concern (Benbya et al., 2024; Chowdhury et al., 2024), yet the feasibility assessment reveals that the process through which novices develop such capability is itself at risk. If GenAI’s default operation erodes the cognitive work through which novices develop judgement (Doshi & Hauser, 2024; Lee et al., 2025), the long-term consequence is fewer professionals capable of independent judgement. Without that capacity, even DP3’s verification Mechanisms lack a competent human to operate them.

[Discussion paragraph 8] The concern is not reliance on decision support but that ungoverned reliance displaces the development of judgement itself. Governing GenAI-mediated knowledge transfer therefore addresses both preserving what current experts know and ensuring the pipeline producing future decision-makers is not undermined by the systems meant to support it.

Yet existing governance discourse centres on compliance and risk controls for AI outputs (e.g. Benbya et al., 2024), and recent decision support work defines admissibility conditions that outputs must satisfy before entering decision work (Saup et al., 2026). Both presuppose that trustworthy knowledge inputs exist. When tacit expertise is lost through turnover, no admissibility framework can govern what was never captured. Addressing this foundational gap requires governing the upstream processes through which expert knowledge is captured and preserved as an organisational resource. Accordingly, extending our prior problem theory (O’Sullivan et al., 2025) through Echeloned Design Science Research (eDSR) (Tuunanen et al., 2024), this paper derives and validates four design principles (DPs) for governing GenAI-mediated tacit knowledge transfer. title: Understanding What Design Research Is and Is NOT
authors: Gibson, Michael R. (1); Owens, Keith M. (1)
abstract: This chapter distinguishes legitimate design research from superficial inquiry, building on Cross‚Äôs definition as the 'development, articulation, and communication of design knowledge.' We critique 'swimming in the shallow end'‚Äîsuperficial approaches like basic interviews that generate only situational knowledge without systematic inquiry or hypothesis testing. We challenge 'faux design research' that misapplies scientific methods, creating false validity impressions while failing to assess improved sense-making or usability. Authentic design research employs methodologies like 'failure analysis' (examining the evolution of World War II prototypes and Ukrainian drone development are provided as examples) and 'research through design' that positions entire design processes as research enterprises. We argue that design research must prioritize practical relevance over traditional scientific validity, requiring evidence-based rationales, hypothesis testing, and systematic methodologies that advance theoretical understanding and practical application. ¬© 2026 selection and editorial matter, Michael R. title: A design theory for data quality tools in data ecosystems: Findings from three industry cases
authors: Altendeitering, M. (1, 2); Guggenberger, T.M. (1, 2); MoÃàller, F. (1, 3)
abstract: Data ecosystems are a novel inter-organizational form of cooperation. They require at least one data provider and one or more data consumers. Existing research mainly addresses generativity mechanisms in this relationship, such as business models or role models for data ecosystems. However, an essential prerequisite for thriving data ecosystems is high data quality in the shared data. Without sufficient data quality, sharing data might lead to negative business consequences, given that the information drawn from them or services built on them might be incorrect or produce fraudulent results. We tackle this issue precisely since we report on a multi-case study deploying data quality tools in data ecosystem scenarios. From these cases, we derive generalized prescriptive design knowledge as a design theory to make the knowledge available for others designing data quality tools for data sharing. Subsequently, our study contributes to integrating the issue of data quality in data ecosystem research and provides practitioners with actionable guidelines inferred from three real-world cases. title: Socio-technical design principles for a multi-stakeholder agriculture extension information system in Ethiopia
authors: Atinaf, M. (1); Anteneh, S. (1); Kifle, M. (1)
abstract: Though the importance of design knowledge is becoming critical the effort to producing prescriptive knowledge in the information and communication for development domain is limited. Such knowledge addresses the requirements of local development contexts, practices, and priorities and balance the issues of generalizability and specificity through contextualizing the design principles (DPs). This research shows how such contextualized design knowledge can be produced to address the challenges presented by socio-technical contexts in the Ethiopian agriculture extension information service system. The knowledge produced is grounded on insights from kernel/reference theories and complemented with knowledge from empirical observations. The research identified six DPs to address four design requirements: (1) supporting stakeholders' networking; (2) developing information content that meets the needs of the local context; (3) improving accessibility of agricultural extension information to stakeholders; and (4) enhancing adoption and effective use of the information system. The DPs to address the above socio-technical requirements fall into principles to improve: (1) actor-to-actor networking; (2) agriculture extension information system content development; (3) the accessibility of information; (4) device accessibility; (5) accessibility of interfaces; and (6) effective use and adoption of the (AEIS).

title: A domain reference ontology for design science research knowledge bases
authors: Piest, Jean Paul Sebastian (1); Jales de Oliviera, Victor Benoiston (2); de Alencar Silva, Patr√≠cio (2); Ricardo da Cunha, Manoel (2); van Sinderen, Marten (1)
abstract: Knowledge bases play an important role in Design Science Research (DSR). Although some related work exists, no domain reference ontology is available for reuse and cumulative development of design knowledge. This paper presents a domain reference ontology for DSR knowledge bases using the SABiO methodology. The domain reference ontology is designed in Visual Paradigm based on the UFO and represented in OntoUML. Stakeholders have verified the results and syntactic consistency has been checked using the OntoUML plugin in Visual Paradigm. An operational ontology was designed and implemented in a DSR project in OWL using WebProt√©g√©. The ontology was populated and validated using competency questions and expert opinion. The domain reference ontology provides a blueprint for DSR knowledge bases and a conceptual foundation to develop operational ontologies to support the cumulative development of design knowledge. Current work aims to evaluate the use of the operational ontology within operational systems and implement an interactive website for online collaboration. A user group and working group will be established to manage the lifecycle, which is open for interested scholars and professionals to join. ¬© 2024 Copyright for this paper by its authors.

title: Using Schema Theory to Construct the Designer's Knowledge Framework
authors: Guo, S. (1)
abstract: Design knowledge is often categorized as tacit knowledge, but it is not randomly scattered within the designer's mind. Schema is used to capture the designer's knowledge, representing the structure of knowledge. This study explores the formation and use of design knowledge among designers through in-depth interviews, analysis of precedent works, and design task testing, outlining the structure of design knowledge frameworks. The research reveals that design knowledge is stored in the form of conceptual schema at different levels. The classification of conceptual schema is closely intertwined with the design process and outcomes. The structural framework of conceptual schema plays a crucial role in the retrieval of design knowledge, with different types of conceptual schemas able to retrieve different knowledge.

title: A Typology of Knowledge Creation in Design Science Research Projects
authors: Chatterjee, S. (1); Bhattacherjee, A. (2); Gilb, T. (3)
abstract: There is considerable debate in IS-DSR community about what constitutes design knowledge and whether they are best represented as design principles or design theories. While designing an IT artifact to solve a problem addresses the 'how' question, we agree that the 'why' question (why did this work?) is important to answer. This essay attempts to shed light on two fundamental questions in this domain: (1) What are the various ways that knowledge contribution in DSR can be stated? (2) What pathways exists to create and re(use) knowledge in DSR projects? We present a simple 2‚Äâ√ó‚Äâ2 framework that explains with concrete examples four knowledge types - design principles, design attribute postulates, design theories, and good design practices from DSR projects. We further present a dual-use of design knowledge framework that shows pathways and re(use) of knowledge in DSR. We think that this typology is a useful theoretical contribution that can benefit both academics and practitioners conducting DSR projects.

title: Toward a Method for Design Science Research Meta-Studies to Improve the Reusability of Design Principles
authors: Khosrawi-Rad, B. (1); Grogorick, L. (1); Strohmann, T. (1); Robra-Bissantz, S. (1)
abstract: Design science research (DSR) is an established approach to create innovative artifacts while considering relevance and rigor. Design principles (DPs) are the most common knowledge contribution. However, DPs are often not reused in the research and practice community. In this methodology paper, we propose an approach to conduct meta-studies for design knowledge to tackle the issue of low DP reusability. We developed a process model for meta-studies based on DSR-specific terms, evaluated it in an expert workshop with DSR scholars, and demonstrated its application to the example of virtual reality literature. Our meta-study model contributes to the research discussion on the reusability of DPs and is a new approach to gain more transparency about existing DPs in research and practice, enabling the community to better guide future research.

title: Using Schema to Inform Method Design Practices
authors: Chivukula, Shruthi Sai (1); Gray, Colin M. (2)
abstract: There are many different forms of design knowledge that guide and shape a designer‚Äôs ability to act and realize potential realities. Methods and schemas are examples of design knowledge commonly used by design researchers and designers alike. In this pictorial, we explore, engage, and describe the role of schemas as tools that can support design researchers in formulating methods to support design action, with our framing of method design specifically focused on ethical design complexity. We present four ways for method designers to engage with schema: 1) Systems to operationalize complex design constructs such as ethical design complexity through an A.E.I.O.YOU schema; 2) Classifiers to map existing methods and identify the possibility for new methods through descriptive semantic differentials; 3) Tools that enable the creation of methods that relate to one or more elements of the schema through creative departures from research to design; and 4) Interactive channels to playfully engage potential and new opportunities through schema interactivity. ¬© 2024, CC BY-NC-ND.

SETTING THE STAGE Design Schema We build upon Nelson and Stolterman’s [12] definition of a design schema as a “means for representing holistic concepts, ideas, and fundamental knowledge in visual form.” Unlike scientific schema, we position the creation and use of schemas as a designerly practice, whereby schema can support the method designer in better understanding the design space, provide a vocabulary to structure or explain problem space traversal, and indicate gaps, opportunities, or combinations of approaches that can be discovered through schema use. We focus on the utility of this approach for design scholars creating new methods to increase ethical engagement, but similar processes or concepts could be taken on by practitioners as well as they seek to appropriate existing methods or build new ones to address concerns in their practice. title: A taxonomy and ontology for digital platforms
authors: Derave, T. (1); Gailly, F. (1); Sales, T.P. (2); Poels, G. (1)
abstract: In academic literature and in business communication digital platforms are categorized into different types including, but not limited to, multi-sided platform, digital marketplace, on-demand platform and sharing economy platform. Observing the substantial literature on these platform types, both in academia and professional contexts, there is lack of consensus on the definition of these digital platform types. On top of that, there is a lack of knowledge in the literature on the requirements and design of these digital platform types. We address the observed lack of shared conceptualizations by creating a taxonomy for digital platforms, by classifying digital platforms according to their distinctive features. Further, we address the lack of design knowledge with a digital platform reference ontology that describes the construction and operation of the different types of digital platform that are distinguished in the taxonomy. This reference ontology, developed using the Unified Foundational Ontology (UFO) and several of its core ontologies, is organized into different ontology modules, allowing to describe functionalities that are common to any digital platform and more specific functionalities that have their origin in the distinctive features that were used to construct the taxonomy. The taxonomy that we contribute helps to clearly define the different types of digital platform that can be observed today. It also helps analysing further evolutions in the platform domain. The contribution of a well-founded digital platform reference ontology is a step towards a better understanding of digital platform functionality, better communication between stakeholders, and eventually may facilitate future research and development of new types of digital platform. All rights reserved Elsevier.
venue: Information Systems
primary_reason: Develops a taxonomy and ontology for digital platforms from literature and addresses lack of shared definitions, satisfying C2 and likely C3; flagged to confirm definitional contribution.
val_reason: The abstract suggests development and evaluation of lawful design patterns to incorporate legal requirements, which may involve categorizing design knowledge, but the abstract does not confirm a literature-based synthesis or explicit definition.
extracted_context:
A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms research we already proposed an extension of the DPO for the digital marketplaces type, with modules to model functionality related to specific business model choices (Derave et al., 2021). We based ourselves on the business model framework of (Täuscher and Laudien, 2018) and the business model ontology of (Osterwalder, 2004), to expand our taxonomy (and ontology) with properties (and modules) like listing type, price discovery and calculation, payment system, revenue stream and source, conversation and review system. This partly solves the lack of research concerning the technological aspects of the development, implementation and operation of platforms (Sutherland and Jarrahi, 2018; Trabucchi, Muzellec and Ronteau, 2019). Further, we also proposed a method where the DPO and its DPO business model extension (for digital marketplaces) is used as the foundation for the development of platform-specific ontologies (Derave et al., 2022b) as demonstrated in section 6. Platform-specific ontologies increase the knowledge of software design in the sharing and platform economy, which triggers the conception of alternatives for monopolistic companies like Airbnb and Uber, who are criticized for paying low wages, taking high commission fees and avoiding taxes (Kenney and Zysman, 2016). Most recently, we proposed a method where a platform-specific ontology can drive Minimum Viable Platform (MVP) development (Derave et al., 2022a). A MVP is a minimum viable product of a digital platform, hence software with just enough features to be usable by early customers who can then provide feedback for future product development (Gracia, 2022). We are now developing several MVPs operating a variety of business models.

A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms confusion, existing digital platforms strive to be under the big tent of the ‘sharing economy’, because of the positive symbolic value of sharing (Frenken and Schor, 2017). This lack of agreement makes it difficult for scholars to determine the impact that digital platforms of different types have on the economy as well as on the society at large. It iss difficult to compare different studies and their results since each of them conceptualises the markets and their platforms differently (Ertz, Durif and Arcand, 2016). Clearer definitions of digital platform types can improve communication, guide future research, and produce useful contributions and recommendations for practitioners who are keen on learning more about the opportunities that digitalization brings for fostering the platform economy (Ertz et al., 2016; Wieringa, 2019). Furthermore, academic research on the functionalities of different digital platform types is scarce. Sutherland and Jarrahi (2018) observe “that perspectives on technology are currently lacking in the research on the sharing economy” as “few research works have been done from the perspective of information technologies” (Yin et al., 2018). During our study, we found out that there is a clear lack of knowledge regarding the requirements and design of the different types of digital platforms. Therefore, it is not known whether existing SaaS tools for developing digital platforms, like opensource Sharetribe Go (Sharetribe, 2022) for digital marketplaces, support the functionalities expected of other platform types too.

A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms only supports the creation of OntoUML models; it can automatically verify if they comply with the syntactic constraints of the language. The plugin uses colours to indicate the ontological nature of the individuals that instantiate each class: objects are represented in pink, reified relationships are represented in green, aspects of individuals are represented in blue, events are represented in yellow, and high-order types are represented in purple. In step 3 of the ontology engineering process (Figure 2), ontology patterns from reference ontologies are used to model the requirements defined in step 2. Inherent in the use of OntoUML is the Unified Foundational Ontology (UFO) which has been purposefully designed as an ontological foundation for conceptual modelling. For representing general (i.e., not domainspecific) modelling situations, we therefore employ Foundational Ontology Patterns (FOPs) from OntoUML/UFO. We also use as reference ontology UFO-S, a core ontology for services that provides a clear account of service-related concepts (Nardi et al., 2015), such as service offering, service negotiation, service agreement, and service delivery. As digital platforms provide a service to their users, we consider the platform economy as part of the service economy. Therefore, we use Domain-Related Ontology Patterns (DROPs) from UFO-S to represent service domainspecific modelling situations. We illustrate the use of UFO FOPs in Figure 3.

A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms 2.2 Ontology engineering method Our second design artefact is the Digital Platform Ontology (DPO), which is based on our taxonomy and built by combining the methods of Ruy et al. (2017) and Falbo (2014). Figure 2: Ontology engineering method Four steps are distinguished in the ontology engineering process (Figure 2): 1. The first step is to segment our domain of interest into subdomains that can be modelled independently, as designing ontologies in a modular manner achieves more efficient ontology management and change handling (Ensan and Du, 2013). Since we started building the ontology after creating the taxonomy, we modularize the DPO based on the digital platform properties as defined in the taxonomy. 2. The second step is to define ontology requirements. First, using the working definition that guided taxonomy development, we define the requirements related to the functionality provided by any digital platform, independent of its taxonomic classification. Second, specific requirements for each ontology module are defined based on both the literature and our sample used in the taxonomy development. For the combination of ontology modules that apply to an individual digital platform, these requirements define what functionality is expected for the digital platform to be defined as an instance of a certain digital platform type. 8

A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms specific ontologies using Airbnb and Uber Eats as examples. In section 7 we discuss the evaluation of the ontology. In section 8 we further discuss the implications of our results and outline our current and future research. Finally, in section 9, we conclude by stating the novel contribution of this paper. 2 Methodology This paper presents two interrelated artefacts that were developed using the Design Science Research (DSR) process. For developing the taxonomy, we followed the method of Nickerson, Varshney and Muntermann (2013), which is explained in sub-section 2.1. For developing the DPO, we combined the ontology engineering methods of Falbo (2014) and Ruy et al. (2017), which is presented in sub-section 2.2. Finally, in sub-section 2.3, we motivate our choice of tool for developing the DPO, which is OntoUML (Guizzardi, 2005; Guizzardi et al., 2021), and the reference ontologies we use as a foundation for constructing the DPO. 2.1 Taxonomy development method Figure 1 shows the process we followed for creating the digital platform taxonomy. This process is guided by the taxonomy development method of Nickerson, Varshney and Muntermann (2013). 5

discussion_full:
[Discussion paragraph 1] One of the main issues in ontology engineering is the knowledge acquisition bottleneck (Starr and Parente De Oliveira, 2013). This bottleneck is observed in the digital platform domain where the meaning of concepts can change very fast. This may create two problems. First, it may be the case that just after the ontology is ready to be deployed, the digital platform conceptualisation has already changed. Second, it is possible that the cost to update the ontology is prohibitively high given the fast development of the field. We postulate, however, that our ontology can accommodate changes well because of its modular structure, which is based on the taxonomic structure. Should new digital platform types emerge, then it needs to be investigated if they can be defined in terms of the taxonomic structure presented in the paper. If the present taxonomic structure allows defining new types, then the ontology doesn’t need to change, as the new types can be modelled by a combination of existing ontology modules. In case of changes to the taxonomic structure, for instance, because of properties that are conceptualized as new values for 46

[Discussion paragraph 2] A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms existing dimensions or even values on new dimensions, then we recommend to first define new ontology requirements for these new properties, and next develop new ontology modules following the approach set forth in this paper. We wish to emphasise that to keep control over the further development of the ontology, the alignment of taxonomy and ontology is key. Despite this issue, we believe that the taxonomy and ontology created in this paper can already be important artefacts to grab and communicate the complexity and future direction of the digital platform domain. We noticed during this research, for instance, that different digital platform types are starting to merge, combining providers of different sorts on one platform for a single offering. A first example is the car service app Uber, that is further being developed to allow combining different types of providers and products into one transaction (Karanovic, 2019). When someone wants to have transportation from A to B, Uber is planning to propose a combination of Uber car, train, plane, bike and scooter renting services into one service to get the customers to their destination in the cheapest way possible. Another example is a travel platform as described by (Derave, 2019) and Radonjic-Simic, Richter and Pfistere (2019), where multiple assets, goods and services (including accommodation, car, activities, guide, and driver) by different providers can be combined as one offering on the platform. These combinations of platforms are also called mega-platforms (Radonjic-Simic et al., 2019), hybrid platforms (Acquier et al., 2019) or integrated platforms (P. C. Evans and Gawer, 2016), that provide the functionalities of different platform types.

[Discussion paragraph 3] As shown by these examples, the taxonomy can help to analyse such evolutions by classifying digital platforms according to the distinctive properties of the platform types that are included, and our ontology can help to understand and communicate these distinctive properties. In this paper, we modelled both the general functionality of a digital platform, independent of its type, and the functionality that differs depending on the type of digital platform. In follow-up 47

[Discussion paragraph 4] A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms research we already proposed an extension of the DPO for the digital marketplaces type, with modules to model functionality related to specific business model choices (Derave et al., 2021). We based ourselves on the business model framework of (Täuscher and Laudien, 2018) and the business model ontology of (Osterwalder, 2004), to expand our taxonomy (and ontology) with properties (and modules) like listing type, price discovery and calculation, payment system, revenue stream and source, conversation and review system. This partly solves the lack of research concerning the technological aspects of the development, implementation and operation of platforms (Sutherland and Jarrahi, 2018; Trabucchi, Muzellec and Ronteau, 2019). Further, we also proposed a method where the DPO and its DPO business model extension (for digital marketplaces) is used as the foundation for the development of platform-specific ontologies (Derave et al., 2022b) as demonstrated in section 6. Platform-specific ontologies increase the knowledge of software design in the sharing and platform economy, which triggers the conception of alternatives for monopolistic companies like Airbnb and Uber, who are criticized for paying low wages, taking high commission fees and avoiding taxes (Kenney and Zysman, 2016). Most recently, we proposed a method where a platform-specific ontology can drive Minimum Viable Platform (MVP) development (Derave et al., 2022a). A MVP is a minimum viable product of a digital platform, hence software with just enough features to be usable by early customers who can then provide feedback for future product development (Gracia, 2022). We are now developing several MVPs operating a variety of business models.

[Discussion paragraph 5] A test case is set up with aspiring entrepreneurs who plan to develop a MVP of their platform idea starting from a self-constructed platform-specific ontology. A clear method for MVP development is important, because due to high costs of implementation, competitors with less diversification but superior technology are still capable of monopolizing a market. Lowering the barrier of digital platform development is 48

[Discussion paragraph 6] A Taxonomy and Ontology for Digital Platforms therefore vital as this creates incentives to enter and isolate the influence of large competitors, increasing the differentiation of digital platforms. It is therefore in our opinion vital to facilitate the development of alternative and more socially responsible platforms. In future research, we plan to further validate and expand our artefacts. First, we will further validate our taxonomy and ontology with domain experts. At this moment, we are in contact with a consultant firm with expertise in the agile development of digital platforms. This firm applies short iterations to gather the requirements from the client (i.e., the future platform company) and develop software in accordance with these requirements. We plan to validate if the application of our ontology is able to capture these requirements and test how it can help to elicit these requirements from the client more adequately. Second, we will implement our ontology using the Web Ontology Language (OWL) (W3C OWL Working Group, 2012) by extending gUFO (Almeida et al., 2019), an implementation of UFO for the Semantic Web. This would allow us to create a knowledge graph with data about digital platforms instances, a resource that could be leveraged by both researchers and practitioners. This activity would have the side benefit of further validating our ontology, as the representation of more cases would stress-test the ontological commitments we made there. Another avenue for further research is to extend the taxonomy and ontology. First, we plan to capture the possible influence of regulation, as a 'one size fits all' policy and regulatory approach is inappropriate (Codagnone et al., 2016).

[Discussion paragraph 7] Applying DROPs of UFO-L, a core ontology that accounts for a comprehensive set of related legal notions, including rights and duties, no-rights and permissions, powers and liabilities, disabilities and immunities, as well as liberties (Griffo, Almeida, Guizzardi and Nardi, 2019) can be an aide in this matter. 49

We based ourselves on the business model framework of (Täuscher and Laudien, 2018) and the business model ontology of (Osterwalder, 2004), to expand our taxonomy (and ontology) with properties (and modules) like listing type, price discovery and calculation, payment system, revenue stream and source, conversation and review system. Further, we also proposed a method where the DPO and its DPO business model extension (for digital marketplaces) is used as the foundation for the development of platform-specific ontologies (Derave et al., 2022b) as demonstrated in section 6. Platform-specific ontologies increase the knowledge of software design in the sharing and platform economy, which triggers the conception of alternatives for monopolistic companies like Airbnb and Uber, who are criticized for paying low wages, taking high commission fees and avoiding taxes (Kenney and Zysman, 2016). Most recently, we proposed a method where a platform-specific ontology can drive Minimum Viable Platform (MVP) development (Derave et al., 2022a). A MVP is a minimum viable product of a digital platform, hence software with just enough features to be usable by early customers who can then provide feedback for future product development (Gracia, 2022). title: A principle-based philosophical framework for fashion design praxis and education in new design landscapes
authors: Harvey, N. (1); Ankiewicz, P. (2)
abstract: Like technology and technology education (Ankiewicz, 2019a), fashion design as a discipline lacks a scientifically founded, discipline-specific philosophical framework that may hold affordances for fashion designers, research scholars, and curriculum developers. Attempting to develop an autonomous theory for fashion design might be overly ambitious, so we do not propose a new theory; instead, we superimpose Mitcham's (1994) philosophical framework of technology onto Love's (2000) meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory for the application to fashion design praxis. The purpose of this conceptual article is to conceptualise a philosophical framework for fashion design to be considered by fashion designers, researchers and curriculum developers when engaging in praxis. The relevant research questions are then: what are the underlying philosophical principles for fashion design within the current approach to praxis? What are the affordances of such a principle-based philosophical framework for fashion designers in their practice; researchers, scholars and curriculum developers? Within the current design praxis, this article proposes a principle-based philosophical framework including volition (mind-set), design knowledge (epistemology), design process (methodology) and objects (ontology). This framework may well create opportunities for fashion designers to consider these related principles and approach their praxis in alternative ways. Similarly, researchers wanting to strengthen scholarship for fashion design could use each of these different principles as units of analysis to explore and introduce new perspectives and research about the praxis. Alternative volition and new forms of design knowledge have the potential to change designers' choices, reasoning, cognition, and design processes and scholars may well write about this also. Finally, the framework offers the potential for scholars to question and research the ontological nature of objects and the relationship between designer, intentions and artefact function when sustainability and design for social good come into play. Furthermore, the framework for fashion design will also inform curriculum and programme development in fashion design education.
venue: International Journal of Technology and Design Education

Love organises the third, design process level into four sub-tiers, namely, mechanisms of choice, design methods, design process structure and theories about the internal processes of designers and collaboration. Mechanisms of choice refer to why selections are made between “different objects, processes, or systems and how solutions are measured” (Love, 2000, p. 305). On the following sub-tier, design methods address theories and proposals for describing methods and techniques. As a creative process, design methods afford designers the opportunity to use a number of different “associative and analogical techniques”, visual creativity and manipulation of ideas, depending on designers’ experience (Love, 2000, p. 310). This leads the way to the succeeding design process sub-tier, which focuses on structure and models. For the final sub-tier, Love (2000, p. 306) argues for theories about designers’ “reasoning and cognition”, collaborative design team negotiation, and the “cultural effects on designers’ output”. As information processors, designers appear to select and conFig. 1 Meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory adapted from Love (2000) 1 3

N. Harvey, P. Ankiewicz Fig. 3 Principles for fashion design praxis in the current design landscape (Harvey, 2018; Harvey et al., 2019) tions about the objects, processes and systems nor how elements behave, interact or work together in the designed object. Love names his third level as design process classifying theory into four sub-tiers of: (1) mechanisms of choice, (2) design methods, (3) design process structures and (4) theories about the internal processes of designers and collaboration. Mechanisms of choice theorise about designers making certain choices and selections in the design process. For example, fashion designers may choose to design a dress (object) as opposed to shirt. Yet, in Mitcham’s framework, theoretical elements of mechanisms of choice are reflected in volition. Theories about design methods (second sub-tier in Love’s framework) exist to study the design process yet in Mitcham’s framework such theoretical elements about design methods lacks detail and is simply characterised as activity. Turning to Love’s third sub-tier, several design process structures (models) outlining different design stages exist but Mitcham’s framework does not suggest any theoretical elements. Rather, Mitcham’s framework is very specific to technology, and only shows a simplistic four-mode process in which volition and knowledge in humans give rise to activities which result in objects. Then again, Mitcham links technology activity to both designing and manufacture, but we make the distinction that they are two different processes.

A principle-based philosophical framework for fashion design praxis and… nect data in a rational manner, but in creative procedures “intuition, experience, feelings and style” are core to the designer’s internal processes and collaboration (Love, 2000, p. 311). On the fourth hierarchal level, Love (2000, p. 307) positions what he calls “philosophical matters”, including general design theories, epistemology and ontology. He contends that general design theories are about the all-inclusive activity of designing and its association with objects. Epistemology of design theory analyses and discusses the “critical study of the nature, grounds, limits and criteria or validity of design knowledge” and ontology associates human values, researchers’ core assumptions and theoretical critique (Love, 2000, p. 306). Mitcham’s philosophical framework of technology In contrast to Love’s, general meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory Mitcham’s (1994) philosophical framework is specific to technology praxis and includes the four modes of volition, knowledge, activity and objects as illustrated in Fig. 2. These four modes of manifestation of technology have been linked to the four components of general philosophy, as well as to the analytical tradition within the philosophy of technology, namely ontology, epistemology, methodology and volition respectively (Ankiewicz, 2019a; Ankiewicz et al., 2006, Hallström & Ankiewicz, 2019), as illustrated in Fig. 2. On the one hand, technological knowledge and volition, with their origin within human beings, give rise to technological activities expressed as concrete technological objects (indicated by the black arrows). On the other hand, objects can also influence peoples’ activities, knowledge and their will (indicated by the grey arrows).

A principle-based philosophical framework for fashion design praxis and… working definitions for design and fashion design praxis, the discussion turns to the research methodology employed in this article. Research methodology With the purpose of the article and these research questions in mind, we consider how the current approach to design could possibly be developed to create units of analysis for scholars to throw fresh light on research for and about fashion design praxis, and for fashion designers themselves to consider. To achieve this, a qualitative meta-synthesis was done of the four modes of the philosophical framework of technology defined by Carl Mitcham (1994), serving as a lens to analyze the meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory discussed by Terrence Love (2000) for application to fashion design praxis. Love (2000) established a meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory which is not explicitly philosophical by nature. Mitcham (1994) established a preliminary philosophical framework of technology. Mitcham’s four modes of manifestation of technology have been innovatively linked to the four components of general philosophy, as well as to the analytical approach within the philosophy of technology, namely ontology, epistemology, methodology and volition respectively (Ankiewicz, 2019a; Ankiewicz et al., 2006; De Vries, 2017). Design is a common key tenet of both technology (De Vries, 2018) and fashion design. Thus, Mitcham’s philosophical framework of technology can serve as a bridge between design theory and technology by linking the two concepts philosophically. By superimposing Mitcham’s (1994) philosophical framework onto Love’s (2000) meta-theoretical taxonomy a ‘philosophy modulation’ of the latter is achieved.

He goes on to claim that these activities are either found in operational production processes or design actions, which use intentional planning and both ‘knowing that’ and ‘knowing how’ to solve problems. The final mode in Mitcham’s framework is technology as object grounded in ontology or the nature of the object (Ankiewicz et al., 2006). He maintains that technology manifests itself in objects that are materialised through human construction by way of tools associated with doing or making actions. For Mitcham, clothes, utensils, structures, tools and machines are the actual objects, with each having its individual historical background, features, functions and intrinsic and extrinsic uses depending on the social contexts in which it is used. Having outlined Love’s meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory and Mitcham’s philosophical framework of technology, the discussion shifts to the findings that emerged from the ‘philosophy modulation’. Findings Superimposing Mitcham’s modes of technology onto Love’s meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory Against the aforementioned backdrop, we superimpose the theoretical elements in Mitcham’s four modes of the manifestation of technology onto Love’s meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory, as a point of departure. To achieve this goal, we compare the four main levels and sub-tiers in Love’s hierarchy (Fig. 1) with Mitcham’s four main modes of manifestation of technology (Fig. 2). Drawing from these respective figures, in Table 1, we show how Mitcham’s specific framework for technology praxis is superimposed onto Love’s meta-theoretical taxonomy. 1 3

A principle-based philosophical framework for fashion design praxis and… (methodology) and objects (ontology). This framework may well create opportunities for fashion designers to consider these related principles and approach their praxis in alternative ways. Similarly, researchers wanting to strengthen scholarship for fashion design could use each of these different principles as units of analysis to explore and introduce new perspectives and research about the praxis. They may, for example, write about the volition of fashion designers and how this influences and change praxis. It is also significant that current fashion design knowledge may no longer suffice in the current design landscape, and, with alternative volition coming into play, new forms of conceptual and procedural knowledge and understanding may well inform praxis. This creates the possibility of broadening the scope of research in design knowledge for and about fashion design praxis. Alternative volition and new forms of design knowledge have the potential to change designers’ choices, reasoning, cognition, and design processes and scholars may well write about this also. Finally, object as the last principle offers the potential for scholars to question and research the ontological nature of objects and the relationship between designer, intentions and artefact function when sustainability and design for social good come into play. Furthermore, the principle-based philosophical framework for fashion design will also inform curriculum and programme development in fashion design education.

This process is metaphorically similar to the concept of frequency modulation in physics, where the characteristics of one system (here, Love’s taxonomy) are changed by impressing another system (here, Mitcham’s philosophical framework) on it. While acknowledging that there are diverse sub-sections in fashion research and praxis such as production, distribution, merchandising, and retail, in this article, we concentrate solely on design praxis. We have specifically chosen Mitcham’s framework for technology to assist in identifying the principles mentioned above as it is already philosophically grounded in engineering (including technology) design. Investigating Love’s meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory and Mitcham’s philosophical framework of technology In this section, we outline Love’s (2000) meta-theoretical taxonomy for design theory, which he defines on four core levels: (1) direct perception of realities, (2) objects, (3) design process, and (4) philosophical matters. We also outline Mitcham’s (1994) philosophical framework of technology, which he defines as the four modes of the manifestation of technology: (1) volition, (2) knowledge, (3) activity, and (4) objects. Love’s taxonomy for design theory offers a structure to classify and analyze design theories. Mitcham’s (1994) philosophical framework – as a broad, preliminary conceptual framework of what technology is (Svenningsson, 2019) – consists of the four modes in which technology manifests in praxis when combined in a certain way. 1 3

Second principle: Design knowledge with its epistemological implications for fashion design praxis The second principle we identify is design knowledge with its epistemological implications for fashion design praxis. Design knowledge is knowledge held and used by designers 1 3

For researchers writing for and about fashion design, design knowledge creates research potential to bring new perspectives on how knowledge manifests in praxis, and how it intersects with design process activities, as well as how volition supports and reflects knowledge. Third principle: Design process and its theoretical elements for alternative praxis With design knowledge intersecting with design activities, design process, with its theoretical elements, is the third proposed principle for fashion design praxis. Cross (1984, p. vii) confirms that design process is the “study of the principles, practices and procedures of design” in relation to how designing is and could be carried out. Turning to fashion design, scholars have put forward design process models and have written about the activities in each stage but it seems as if inspiration is primary and the first stage in the design process. However, with alternative and current approaches to design praxis, design process models have seen a shift to co-design models such as the “fuzzy front end” (Sanders & Stappers, 2008, p. 6). The fuzzy front end of the model proposes that qualitative, as opposed to quantitative, empirical data are collected by way of open-ended questions posed to users in order to generate design criteria and ideas. Also, with this model there is collaboration between designer and user because the user is an active engaging participant throughout the design process stages of conceptualisation, prototype and product development. With this in mind, new design process activities and models have the potential to change the scope of fashion design praxis by allowing for users’ voices, as opposed to them being merely passive recipients of the work of the lone designer genius.

title: Contestable AI by Design: Towards a Framework
authors: Alfrink, Kars (1); Keller, Ianus (2); Kortuem, Gerd (1); Doorn, Neelke (3)
abstract: As the use of AI systems continues to increase, so do concerns over their lack of fairness, legitimacy and accountability. Such harmful automated decision-making can be guarded against by ensuring AI systems are contestable by design: responsive to human intervention throughout the system lifecycle. Contestable AI by design is a small but growing field of research. However, most available knowledge requires a significant amount of translation to be applicable in practice. A proven way of conveying intermediate-level, generative design knowledge is in the form of frameworks. In this article we use qualitative-interpretative methods and visual mapping techniques to extract from the literature sociotechnical features and practices that contribute to contestable AI, and synthesize these into a design framework. ¬© The Author(s) 2022.
venue: Minds and Machines
